title: [lora_l2.c:366] 1-second backoff on RX error does not account for thread abort timing
description:
Final pass: This was missed because reviewers focused on the consecutive_errors counter and warning threshold, not the sleep interaction with abort.

Root cause: When lora_recv() returns a non-timeout error, the thread sleeps for 1 second:
  k_sleep(K_MSEC(1000));

The stop() function at line 602-616 uses RX_THREAD_QUICK_JOIN_MS (100ms) + RX_TIMEOUT_MS as join timeout. If the thread is in this 1-second backoff sleep when stop() is called:
1. Thread is sleeping for 1000ms
2. stop() waits 100ms + RX_TIMEOUT_MS (default 1000ms)
3. Total potential wait: up to 1100ms after state change
4. But backoff can last full 1000ms, which is within timeout - close margin

If RX_TIMEOUT_MS is configured lower (e.g., 100ms for fast response), the join timeout becomes 200ms and the 1000ms backoff will cause forced abort.

Impact: On persistent LoRa errors, stop() may force-abort the thread unnecessarily because the backoff sleep exceeds the join timeout. This triggers the ABORTED state and requires full deinit/init cycle.

Fix: Either:
1. Make backoff interruptible (use k_sleep with cancellation or poll state in backoff loop)
2. Add backoff duration to join timeout calculation
3. Document the RX_TIMEOUT_MS must be >= 1000ms when error backoff is possible
close_reason: Fixed: Added comprehensive documentation in lora_l2.c rx_thread explaining the 1000ms backoff timing interaction with stop() join timeout, including analysis of why forced abort during error backoff is acceptable behavior for a malfunctioning radio.
